one_line_takeaway: Ba-Logic shows that clean-label graph backdoor attacks fail primarily because injected triggers are not treated as important by the GNN's own prediction logic, and fixes this by explicitly optimizing trigger importance (via a sensitivity-analysis-based logic-poisoning loss) together with uncertainty-based poisoned-node selection, pushing ASR from the 20-70% range achieved by prior clean-label baselines to near 100% across GCN/GAT/GIN and multiple graph tasks while preserving clean accuracy.
key_finding: On node classification with |VP|=100-300 clean-label poisoned nodes, prior clean-label-adapted methods plateau at low ASR on Pubmed (Table 1: ERBA ~22-23%, GTA-C ~38-39%, UGBA-C ~71%, DPGBA-C ~64%), and IRT on Cora stays low (Table 2: e.g. ERBA 12.3% at k=3 vs UGBA-C 42.4% at k=3), consistent with Theorem 1's prediction that low trigger-importance rate caps attack success. Ba-Logic instead achieves ASR typically close to 100% across all tested datasets/models in the main comparison (Table 3), e.g. on Cora/GCN 98.52% ASR with 83.59% clean accuracy versus vanilla 83.78% accuracy, versus best baseline UGBA-C at 68.32% ASR/79.97% clean-acc; on Flickr/GIN Ba-Logic reaches 100.0% ASR versus DPGBA-C 68.12%; while clean accuracy stays close to the vanilla (unattacked) model's accuracy, unlike baselines which show larger accuracy drops. Against existing defenses on Arxiv (Table 5), Ba-Logic enhances ASR by 'at least 30%' over baselines (e.g. under RIGBD, Ba-Logic 93.23% vs UGBA-C 0.19%, DPGBA-C 0.01%, ECGBA 0.01%). Against the authors' own four adaptive defenses (ER, GM, CD, SAM) on Arxiv, Ba-Logic ASR exceeds 80% in all cases (e.g. ER 80.06%, GM 92.36%, CD 85.38%, SAM 81.09%), all clearly higher than the compared baselines under the same defenses. Ablations (Fig. 5) show both the poisoned-node selector and the logic-poisoning loss individually and jointly contribute to ASR and reduce variance; removing the logic loss (Ba-Logic\T) causes the largest drop. Generalization results show high ASR on graph classification (MUTAG/NCI1/PROTEINS, Fig. 4b), edge prediction (Cora/CS/Physics, Fig. 4c), heterophilous graphs (Table 4, e.g. Squirrel/GCN 99.07%), and diverse surrogate-target model combinations (Fig. 4a heatmap, generally >90%, with GraphSAGE/GraphSAINT somewhat more robust, attributed to node sampling diluting trigger impact).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Ba-Logic, a clean-label graph backdoor attack against GNNs that poisons the model's *inner prediction logic* rather than merely injecting triggers. The authors first show empirically (Table 1: ASR|clean-acc on Pubmed) and via a proposed metric, Important Rate of Triggers (IRT, Table 2 on Cora), plus a GNNExplainer visualization (Fig. 2) and a theoretical bound (Theorem 1), that existing clean-label-adapted graph backdoor methods (GTA-C, UGBA-C, DPGBA-C, ERBA) fail because clean neighbors dominate prediction and injected triggers are treated as unimportant. Building on this diagnosis, Ba-Logic combines two components that are the paper's own design: (1) a poisoned-node selector that scores target-class labeled nodes by an uncertainty metric U(vi) = (1-p(yt|vi)) minus the entropy of the prediction distribution (Eq. 4), selecting the top-Delta_P highest-uncertainty nodes as poisoning sites; and (2) a logic-poisoning trigger generator, an MLP that produces trigger node features and adjacency (Eq. 5) trained with a prediction-logic-poisoning loss (Eq. 7) that forces, via Sensitivity-Analysis gradient-based importance scores (Eq. 6), the trigger nodes' importance to exceed that of clean neighbors by a margin T, plus an unnoticeable constraint (Eq. 8) based on cosine similarity following Dai et al. (2023)'s constraint. The whole system is trained as a bi-level optimization (Eq. 10/11/12) with a surrogate 2-layer GCN.
threat_model: Attacker's goal: by attaching triggers to a subset of training nodes VP of the target class, poison the GNN so that at inference, test nodes with the trigger attached are misclassified as the target class yt, while clean-test accuracy is preserved. Attacker's knowledge/capability (clean-label, black-box): the attacker CANNOT alter node labels (defines the clean-label constraint); can only attach triggers to a subset of already-labeled target-class training nodes VP subset of VL, and at inference can only attach triggers to target test nodes. The target model's architecture/hyperparameters are unknown to the attacker; the attacker trains/optimizes triggers via a surrogate GNN (fixed as a 2-layer GCN in experiments) and transfers the attack to unseen target architectures. This is explicitly stated as a strict black-box limitation, with a threat-model-level comparison to prior work deferred to Appendix B.
limitations: The paper's stated/implied limitations: (1) the attack still requires a surrogate model and unnoticeable-constraint assumptions borrowed from Dai et al. (2023), i.e. it inherits that prior unnoticeability formulation rather than proposing a new one; (2) on Arxiv the ASR of Ba-Logic itself, while still highest, is noted to be affected by 'diverse classes and fixed target-class setting'; (3) sampling-based target architectures (GraphSAGE, GraphSAINT) are 'marginally more robust' against the attack, attributed to sampling diluting trigger importance; (4) against the authors' own adaptive defense ER (explainability regularization), Ba-Logic's ASR drops furthest among the defenses tested (80.06%, the lowest of the four adaptive-defense results), though still far above baselines; (5) the paper explicitly frames itself as an attack paper and defers development of a defense against logic poisoning to future work, noting they see this as 'developing defense strategies that can both defend against logic poisoning and maintain clean accuracy simultaneously through the inverse application of our methodology'; (6) all quantitative claims come from the authors' own experimental setup (their own re-implementation/extension of baselines to clean-label variants with a '-C' suffix), which is a common but self-selected comparison basis.

quantitative_results: Table 1 (Pubmed, |VP|=100): ASR|clean-acc -- ERBA 22.2|85.6, GTA-C 38.4|85.1, UGBA-C 71.1|85.3, DPGBA-C 64.2|85.2
Table 2 (Cora IRT, k=3): ERBA 12.3%, GTA-C 21.0%, UGBA-C 42.4%, DPGBA-C 33.8%
Table 3 (Cora, GCN): Vanilla acc 83.78; Ba-Logic 98.52 ASR | 83.59 clean-acc vs best baseline UGBA-C 68.32 | 79.97
Table 3 (Flickr, GIN): Ba-Logic 100.0 ASR | 46.14 clean-acc vs DPGBA-C 68.12 | 45.09, ERBA 0.00 ASR
Table 3 (Arxiv, GAT): Ba-Logic 98.43 ASR | 65.40 clean-acc vs UGBA-C 71.65 | 65.10
Table 5 (Arxiv, existing defenses): under RIGBD -- ECGBA 0.01, DPGBA-C 0.01, UGBA-C 0.19, Ba-Logic 93.23; under GCN-Prune -- Ba-Logic 96.75 vs UGBA-C 62.31
Table 5 (Arxiv, adaptive defenses): Ba-Logic ASR under ER 80.06, GM 92.36, CD 85.38, SAM 81.09 (all exceed 80%)
Table 4 (heterophilous graphs, GCN): Squirrel 99.07, Chameleon 99.03, Penn 96.34, Genius 98.17 ASR
Fig. 4a (Arxiv, surrogate=GCN transferred to target models): GAT 98.43, GIN 98.18, SAGE 98.45, SAINT 95.21, FastGCN 93.45
baselines_compared: SBA (Zhang et al. 2021) -- mentioned as pioneering random-trigger method, not directly benchmarked in main tables
GTA (Xi et al. 2021), extended to clean-label as GTA-C
UGBA (Dai et al. 2023), extended to clean-label as UGBA-C -- state-of-the-art general backdoor baseline
DPGBA (Zhang et al. 2024b), extended to clean-label as DPGBA-C
EBA (Xu et al. 2021), extended to clean-label as EBA-C
ERBA (Xu & Picek 2022) -- existing clean-label method using Erdos-Renyi random-graph triggers
ECGBA (Fan & Dai 2024) -- existing clean-label method using single-node trigger
SCLBA (Dai & Sun 2025), GCLBA (Meguro et al. 2024), TRAP (Yang et al. 2022) -- graph classification baselines
SNTBA (Dai & Sun 2024), PSO-LB and LB (Zheng et al. 2023) -- edge prediction baselines

Defenses: GCN-Prune (Dai et al. 2023), RobustGCN (Zhu et al. 2019), GNNGuard (Zhang & Zitnik 2020), RIGBD (Zhang et al. 2025); plus 4 adaptive defenses proposed by the authors themselves (ER, GM, CD, SAM)
